name: Enterprise API USCDI Routes
description:
NextGen Enterprise API - USCDI Routes
______________________________________________________
GENERAL USAGE INFORMATION:
______________________________________________________
______________________________________________________
Authentication:
______________________________________________________
No testing capabilities are available via the functions on this documentation page; therefore the "Authorize" button will not function. Additionally, the "Execute" button revealed by selecting the Try it out button in individual routes will produce request code, but will not send a request nor return a response. Enterprise API testing capablities are available via the sandbox referenced below.  Enterprise API Authentication documentation is available from within the material available at https://www.nextgen.com/api. The direct link to this documentation is https://www.nextgen.com/api/EntAPI-GSA-Auth-Docs-Guide.pdf.

3rd Party Developer Organizations or a NextGen Healthcare Clients who have an Enterprise API-powered application in mind and wish to apply to our API Program should visit https://www.nextgen.com/partner-program and choose the "Learn More" link below the most appropriate program description on that page.  Each "Learn More" link leads to the API Partner Program Onboarding form.  The Open Access Partner Program applies solely to NextGen Healthcare Clients.  The Open API Consumer Partner Program and the Premier Partner Program are each applicable solely to 3rd Party Developer Organizations.

Partner applicants can obtain access to the NextGen Developer Portal (which includes additional resources for developers, including a functional Enterprise API sandbox) following submission of an onboarding form and completion of the self-registration process that will be described in the automated email response to onboarding form submissions.
______________________________________________________
ID Naming Conventions:
______________________________________________________
- Unique records (whose values are guids) are all named "id" in the result sets of various Enterprise API USCDI GET Routes;
- However in every other context, the name of an ID is determined by the GET route that returns the ID.

Examples:
GET /persons/lookup "id" values are the expected values for {personId} in all other routes.
GET /persons/{personId}/chart/encounters "id" values are the expected values for {encounterId} in all other routes.
GET /persons/{personId}/chart/allergies "id" values are the expected values for {allergyId} in all other routes.
etc.
______________________________________________________
Start with GET /persons/lookup
______________________________________________________
Almost all routes will require prior knowledge of a valid {personId}.

Please note that the Patient Demographics documentation section contains:

- The GET /persons/lookup route, which is used to obtain one or more {personId} values based on lookup criteria.
- The GET /persons/{personId}/chart/encounters route, which requires a valid {personId} and is used to obtain the {encounterId} value that is required by some (but not all) of the routes in this API.  An encounter is an interaction between a provider and patient that took place at a specific date & time.
______________________________________________________
How to Use OData ($) Parameters
______________________________________________________
Managing Results from NextGen Enterprise API GET Routes:

There are several OData-based "result management" parameters (each beginning with a dollar sign $) that may be used as query string parameters in supported routes.  These OData parameters add valuable functionality & flexibility to many NextGen Enterprise USCDI API GET routes.

If a given GET route supports OData result management, the available parameters for that route will be listed within the route details.

The OData v3.0 Core Protocol (https://www.odata.org/documentation/odata-version-3-0/odata-version-3-0-core-protocol/) has comprehensive details on each of these parameters, but a more focused (and NextGen-specific) explanation of each follows here.
___________________________
$top
___________________________
$top specifies the number of records to be returned (Note: if the $top parameter is omitted from a request, the default set of 25 results are returned; see $skip).

- $top expects a value that is a positive integer between 1 and 1000. The maximum possible number of results per request is 1000, even if the value of $top in a request exceeds 1000 (Obtaining all data from a result set that exceeds 1000 records requires use of $skip).
- Examples:
$top=100 will return up to the 1st 100 results.
$top=1234 will return up to the 1st 1000 results.
___________________________
$skip
___________________________
$skip may be used to ignore the 1st n results.

-$skip expects a value that is a positive integer – ex. $skip=25 will begin the result set at the 26th result.
-$skip is helpful if you want to “paginate” result sets.
-Invoking $skip causes the response set to begin at the n+1 result.
___________________________
$count
___________________________
$count will not return results, but instead return a count of the number of results that would have been returned had $count not been invoked.

- $count=true invokes this function ($count=false is the default state); $count will honor $filter criteria.
- You can (optionally) program your app to use the $count response value in conjunction with additional OData parameters to dictate a path through a logic tree should you choose to define one in your app.  Here are a few examples:

Conditional use of $count to invoke $top - IF n>[$count value], THEN $top=n OR

A $skip series: (e.g. $skip=null, $skip=25, $skip=50, etc.) - A series of responses with results 1-25, 26-50, 51-75, etc. until $skip=[$count value]-25 is reached) OR

A $skip&$top series (e.g. $top=100 [omitting $skip in the 1st request in this series], $skip=100&$top=100, $skip=200&$top=100, etc.) – a series of larger responses with results 1-100, 101-200, and 201-300, etc. until $skip=[$count value]-100 is reached.)
___________________________
$inlinecount
___________________________
$inlinecount returns the result set and will in addition include a total count of all results.

- $inlinecount=allpages invokes this function.
- $inlinecount honors $filter criteria, i.e. the count value will reflect the filtered result set per any criteria applied by using the $filter parameter.
___________________________
$orderby
___________________________
$orderby allows sorting the result set according to the values in a specified response field.

- $orderby expects a value that is a valid data attribute present within the route's response schema.
- The default sorting mode of $orderby is ascending.
- You may specify $orderby=fieldName desc to specify a descending sort order.
___________________________
$filter
___________________________
$filter is a powerful way to limit results by various criteria.

For routes that allow $filter, a syntactically exact sequence is expected that uses logical operators with valid data attributes from the response data schema. The "List of OData $filter Operators" (below) outlines the supported filter functions, including allowed operators, usage/syntax examples, & explanations that pertain to actual routes within the NextGen Enterprise API set.

- The $filter parameter value is generally formatted as:

fieldName logical_operator criteria 

- Specific examples follow in the list of $filter operators.
- Multiple fieldName logical_operator criteria trios can be joined with certain Boolean functions (i.e. and & or). Avoid overly complex/compound use of Boolean conjunctions.
- Most $filter criteria require very specific syntactic usage (see below for $filter usage details & correct examples):

When filtering on a value that is <boolean> (either true or false), that value should be expressed without special syntax, e.g. $filter=fieldName eq true

Criteria that are Strings must be bounded by single quotes, e.g. $filter=fieldName eq 'string_value' 

Criteria that are Guids must include the exact prefix guid followed immediately (no space) by the guid value bounded by single quotes; e.g. fieldName eq guid'00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

Criteria that are date/time strings must be identified by starting the criteria portion with dateTime followed immediately (no space) by a single quote bounded (before and after) declaration of the dateTime value.

- dateTime Usage Examples (also see the “List of OData $filter Operators” below for further examples, including joining multiple dateTime criteria with and & or):

Example 1: fieldName gt dateTime'2020-05-31T23:59:59'
Example 2: fieldName lt datetime'2020-07-01'
______________________________________________________
List of OData $filter Operators:
Details, Syntactic Requirements, and Usage Examples
______________________________________________________
Operator:		eq
___________________________
Description:	Equal to
Usage Example:	GET /endpoint?$filter=fieldName eq true

eq - Returns only the records where the binary attribute has a value of true. Unlike strings (see below), Binary values must not include quote boundaries.
___________________________
Operator:		ne
___________________________
Description:	Not Equal to
Usage Example:	GET /endpoint?$filter=fieldName ne 'string'

ne - This example will return resources where the fieldName value is not "string". Filter criteria that are strings must typically be bounded by single quotes as shown (boolean true/false values are the exception; see "eq" example above).
___________________________
Operator:		gt
___________________________
Description:	Greater Than
Usage Example:	$filter=fieldName gt dateTime'2020-05-31T23:59:59'

gt - Note the syntax is fieldName gt dateTime'YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS'. (T separates the date and time components of the dateTime expression.)  The greater than operator is not the same as "greater than or equal to" (Note: the OData operator ge is not supported by NextGen APIs).
___________________________
Operator:		lt
___________________________
Description:	Less Than
Usage Example:	$filter=fieldName lt datetime'2020-07-01'

lt - Note the syntax is fieldName [operator] dateTime'YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS'. The less than operator is not the same as "less than or equal to" (Note: the OData operator le is not supported by NextGen APIs).
___________________________
Operator:		and
___________________________
Description:	Conjunction
Usage Example:	$filter= fieldName gt dateTime'2020-05-31T23:59:59' and fieldName lt datetime'2020-07-01T00:00:00'

and - Requires each $filter criteria to be true. When used in conjunction with fields that have a dateTime value, “and" is useful in establishing boundaries limiting responses to a specific date range.
___________________________
Operator:		or
___________________________
Description:	Inclusive Disjunction
Usage Example:	$filter= fieldName eq 'string1' or fieldName eq 'string2'

or - Requires only one of the criteria to be true.
___________________________
Operator:		startswith
___________________________
Description:	Requires partial match at string start.
Usage Example:	$filter=startswith(fieldName, 'partial-string')

startswith - This operator is useful when you want to limit responses to the set that share the same beginning (but not entire) value for a given attribute. Note the single quotes are required around the partial string. This example limits the response set to results that have a fieldName value that starts with “partial-string”.
___________________________
Operator:		endswith
___________________________
Description:	Requires partial match at string end.
Usage Example:	$filter=endswith(fieldName, 'partial-string')

endswith - This operator is useful when you want to limit responses to the set that share the same ending (but not entire) value for a given attribute. Note the single quotes are required around the partial string.
___________________________
Operator:		substringof
___________________________
Description:	Requires partial match anywhere within a string.
Usage Example:	$filter=substringof('partial-string', fieldName)

substringof - This operator is useful when you want to limit responses to the set that share the same partial value anywhere within a specific attribute.  Note that the syntax of substringof diverges from startswith & endswith - with substringof, the partial string is defined first and is then followed by the parameter name (whereas the opposite is true with the startswith & endswith functions).
___________________________
___________________________
Expand the USCDI data categories below for individual routes, and select a route to show route-specific details.
